title: High School Reunion

jokes.text: You gave me a dollar ten. Oh, I'm sorry. Too late.
jokes.setup: Someone points out they were overpaid by ten cents
jokes.punchline: John sarcastically apologizes but refuses to give change back
jokes.explanation: Basic petty humor, functional but unremarkable

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm terribly sorry. You caught me red-handed. I've been running this scam for years - overcharging people a dime at a time. It's how I've built my empire.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: But I'm afraid I can't give it back. That dime is spoken for. I've already mentally spent it.

jokes.text: Oh, no. Louie's going to play his favorite song. Oh, no. Every time he's in a bar. Yeah. 'The Green Berets.'
jokes.setup: Characters dread Louie playing his favorite song, expecting something aggressive like 'The Green Berets'
jokes.punchline: He plays 'Feelings' instead - a sappy, emotional ballad
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection that subverts expectations about Louie's character in a surprising way

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You guys want to hear my favorite song?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Oh no, here we go.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Not 'The Green Berets' again.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: No, no, something special. Something that really means something to me.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Brace yourselves.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Nothing like a little 'Feelings' to brighten the day.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Did he just say...

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: He's playing 'Feelings.'

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Feelings... nothing more than feelings...

jokes.text: Why is he playing that song? It's different. It's not any better, but it's different.
jokes.setup: Someone asks why Louie is playing 'Feelings'
jokes.punchline: Brutally honest assessment that it's different from his usual but equally bad
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy in the phrasing with dry honesty

dialogue.speaker: Unknown
dialogue.line: Why are you playing 'Feelings'?

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: It's different from my usual stuff, but it's equally bad.

jokes.text: He's giving me a feeling.
jokes.setup: Louie singing 'Feelings' badly
jokes.punchline: Character plays on the song title to describe their negative reaction
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay that's serviceable but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: Feelings, nothing more than feelings...

dialogue.speaker: Alex Reiger
dialogue.line: Louie, please. Those aren't feelings, those are crimes against music.

jokes.text: With a twist. Gee, I'm sorry. We're out of that.
jokes.setup: Customer orders beer with a twist (unusual request)
jokes.punchline: Bartender claims they're out of twist, treating it like an ingredient
jokes.explanation: Absurd logic treating 'twist' as a physical ingredient rather than a garnish instruction

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: I'd like a beer with a twist.

dialogue.speaker: Bartender
dialogue.line: Sorry, we're all out of twist.

jokes.text: Well, there's a man who knows what he likes.
jokes.setup: Customer just got told the bar is out of what he ordered
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic comment about him 'knowing what he likes' when he can't get it
jokes.explanation: Weak ironic comment that doesn't add much comedic value

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: You know what? I know what I like.

dialogue.speaker: Bartender
dialogue.line: Yeah, and we're out of it.

jokes.text: Hey, uh, Beverly... listen, I got some bad news. I went to the doctor. He told me I only had 40 years to live. I don't know what to do with all that time, so would you go out with me Saturday night?
jokes.setup: Alex starts with 'bad news' from the doctor, setting up expectation of terminal illness
jokes.punchline: Subverts expectation - 40 years is a long time, not short, making his request seem desperate
jokes.explanation: Clever misdirection pickup line that builds false dramatic tension before revealing the absurd twist

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I went to the doctor today. He gave me some bad news.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: He said I only have forty years to live.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Forty years! That's not enough time!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I need at least fifty.

jokes.text: It's because I was kidding around, huh? I have this terrible habit... I like to make girls laugh. Kitchy-kitchy... There, I did it again.
jokes.setup: Alex thinks his joke ruined his chances
jokes.punchline: Immediately proves his point by making another joke (tickling gesture) while trying to be serious
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Alex's compulsive need to be funny even when it hurts him

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I shouldn't have made that joke. I really blew it.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: (makes tickling gesture) Coochie coochie coo!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: See? There I go again.

jokes.text: I mean, if I was a secretary on Wall Street, I wouldn't go out with the stockbrokers. If I was in a factory, I wouldn't go out with factory workers. If I was a nurse in a hospital, I wouldn't go out with the sick people.
jokes.setup: Beverly explaining her rule about not dating within her workplace environment
jokes.punchline: Escalates to comparing doctors to 'sick people' in a hospital
jokes.explanation: Good escalating logic that builds to an absurd false equivalency

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: I have a strict rule about not dating people I work with. It's unprofessional.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: But Beverly, that's ridiculous. Half the people here would date you.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Exactly. And that's why I don't. It's like doctors dating sick people in a hospital.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: What? That doesn't even make sense.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Sure it does. You're surrounded by sick people all day. You can't just start dating them because they're convenient.

jokes.text: No. You mean you wouldn't go out with doctors. Hey, I have my rules, but I'm not crazy.
jokes.setup: Alex corrects Beverly's logic about not dating 'sick people' in hospitals
jokes.punchline: Beverly admits she'd make an exception for rich doctors despite her 'principles'
jokes.explanation: Sharp punchline that exposes Beverly's hypocrisy about her dating rules

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Well, I have my principles. I don't date sick people.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: But they're in a hospital. That's where sick people go.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Well, I'd make an exception for a rich doctor.

jokes.text: Louie, I'll give you a buck, you don't play that song again. Okay.
jokes.setup: Alex offers to pay Louie not to play 'Feelings' again
jokes.punchline: Louie immediately agrees to stop for just one dollar
jokes.explanation: Shows Louie's opportunistic nature - willing to abandon his emotional moment for a quick buck

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Louie, I'll pay you not to play 'Feelings' again.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You got a deal. One dollar.

jokes.text: So what are you losers up to? That was obnoxious? Yeah. Yeah. Okay, okay.
jokes.setup: Louie promises not to be obnoxious, then immediately calls them losers
jokes.punchline: His complete lack of self-awareness about what constitutes being obnoxious
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie moment showing his inability to recognize his own rudeness

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I promise I won't be obnoxious.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You're all a bunch of losers.

jokes.text: That's the first time I ever apologized.
jokes.setup: Louie just apologized for being obnoxious
jokes.punchline: He's amazed at himself for apologizing, like it's a major achievement
jokes.explanation: Good character beat establishing Louie's normally unapologetic nature

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I apologized. Me. Louie De Palma. I said I was sorry. Do you know what this means? I'm growing as a person. I'm becoming a better man. Maybe tomorrow I'll stop scratching myself in public.

jokes.text: So what? I don't go to my high school reunions. Who wants to sit around and watch a bunch of guys sucking in their stomachs?
jokes.setup: Response to Louie not wanting to go to his reunion
jokes.punchline: Cynical but accurate observation about what happens at reunions
jokes.explanation: Relatable observational humor about the vanity at high school reunions

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: You go to your high school reunion, you see all these people you haven't seen in twenty years. Half of them are bald, the other half wish they were. The women all look like their mothers. And everybody lies about what they're doing.

jokes.text: I used to just stand there and wait for the last guy to take his shot.
jokes.setup: Louie describing how everyone picked on him in high school
jokes.punchline: The matter-of-fact way he describes accepting systematic bullying
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that makes Louie's trauma both pathetic and oddly dignified

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: They'd hide my books, trip me in the hallway, steal my lunch money. I didn't mind. I figured, 'Hey, this is what high school is all about.' By senior year, they had me pretty well trained. I'd hand them my money before they even asked.

jokes.text: And the world has never come to an end, but that doesn't mean we root for it to happen.
jokes.setup: Someone wanting to hear Louie's sob stories because he's never opened up before
jokes.punchline: Comparing Louie sharing feelings to the apocalypse
jokes.explanation: Excellent metaphor equating Louie's emotional openness with global catastrophe

dialogue.speaker: Unknown character
dialogue.line: You never talk about your feelings, Louie. I'd like to hear what's really going on inside.

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: You want to hear my feelings? Fine. But I'm warning you - if I start opening up, it's the end of the world as we know it.

jokes.text: Alex, you got something on your shirt. That!
jokes.setup: Louie describing Stanley Tarsus's mean trick
jokes.punchline: Demonstrates the trick by doing it to Alex (presumably flicking his nose when he looks down)
jokes.explanation: Classic show-don't-tell moment that makes the audience experience the prank along with Alex

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You see, Stanley Tarsus pulled this mean trick on me. When someone looks down, you flick 'em right on the nose.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Huh?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Look down.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Just look down for a second.

jokes.text: There was this guy in my high school, Lou Vanelli... He used to tear your chest hair out with his bare hands. Gee, I miss those days.
jokes.setup: Character shares their own high school bully story
jokes.punchline: Nostalgically misses being tortured, suggesting current life is worse
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about nostalgia for simpler times, even when they involved physical pain

dialogue.speaker: Alex Reiger
dialogue.line: You know, I had a bully in high school too. He used to follow me around, push me into lockers, call me names...

dialogue.speaker: Alex Reiger
dialogue.line: But you know what? I'd give anything to go back to those days. At least then someone cared enough to torture me.

jokes.text: Okay, get ready to sob your spleens out. I went to my high school senior prom alone.
jokes.setup: Louie promises a heart-wrenching story
jokes.punchline: The devastating revelation is just going to prom alone, which is sad but not that unusual
jokes.explanation: Sets up expectation for something truly horrible, delivers something merely pathetic

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm gonna tell you a heart-wrenching story.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I went to prom alone.

jokes.text: April love? Is for the very young.
jokes.setup: Louie mentions the prom theme was 'April Love'
jokes.punchline: Someone completes the song lyric, showing knowledge of the Pat Boone song
jokes.explanation: Simple reference humor that relies on audience knowing the song

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: The prom theme was 'April Love'.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: April love, April love, it's like an April day.

jokes.text: I tell them my date was delayed. She couldn't make it until after her Broadway show's curtain came down.
jokes.setup: Louie explaining how he covered for being dateless
jokes.punchline: His obviously fake excuse about dating a Broadway actress
jokes.explanation: Shows Louie's pathetic attempts to save face with grandiose lies

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I told them I was dating a Broadway actress. She's performing eight shows a week, so she's too busy to come to the office. That way, nobody can say she doesn't exist because she's always working.

jokes.text: If you're trying to make me feel better how about being a little bolder?
jokes.setup: Elaine says 'Aw, Louie' sympathetically
jokes.punchline: Louie criticizes her sympathy as insufficient
jokes.explanation: Louie's demanding nature extends even to requesting better sympathy

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Aw, Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Don't 'Aw, Louie' me. I need more than that.

jokes.text: She looked just like a beautician.
jokes.setup: Louie describing how beautiful Sheila looked
jokes.punchline: His idea of the ultimate compliment is comparing someone to a beautician
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Louie's working-class perspective on beauty and glamour

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what I'm gonna tell her? I'm gonna say, 'Sheila, you look like a beautician.'

jokes.text: and I saw they had a high chair there.
jokes.setup: Louie excitedly going to sit with Sheila at prom
jokes.punchline: They put out a high chair to humiliate him about his height
jokes.explanation: Devastating punchline that crystallizes the cruelty Louie faced, perfectly delivered

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: They put out a high chair to humiliate me about my height.

jokes.text: I says to them, 'Okay, you did it. You got me good. Enjoy yourselves.'
jokes.setup: How Louie responded to the high chair humiliation
jokes.punchline: His dignified but defeated acceptance of the cruelty
jokes.explanation: Shows Louie's strange dignity in defeat, making him both pathetic and admirable

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm not sitting in no high chair. I got my pride.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: But if that's what it takes to get the job done... fine. FINE. Bring it on.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Just... everybody pretend you don't see me up here, alright?

jokes.text: and make them all eat crackers, if you know what I mean.
jokes.setup: Louie fantasizing about revenge at the reunion
jokes.punchline: His idea of ultimate dominance is making people eat crackers
jokes.explanation: Hilariously specific and weird revenge fantasy that perfectly captures Louie's mindset

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what I'm gonna do at that reunion? I'm gonna walk in there, and everyone's gonna see how successful I've become. And then you know what my ultimate revenge is gonna be?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm gonna make 'em eat crackers. That's right. Crackers. No cheese, no spread, nothing. Just plain crackers.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: And they're gonna have to swallow 'em dry while I watch and laugh.

jokes.text: I'm not sure being a taxi dispatcher is enough to do the job.
jokes.setup: Louie's grand plans for showing up his classmates
jokes.punchline: Reality check that his actual job won't impress anyone
jokes.explanation: Moment of self-awareness from Louie about his actual status in life

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know, when I go to my high school reunion, I'm gonna walk in there and show all those people who said I wouldn't amount to anything. They're gonna see I'm a successful dispatcher!

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Baccalieri
dialogue.line: Louie, you're a dispatcher at a taxi garage in New York.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Exactly! I'm the boss!

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Baccalieri
dialogue.line: Nobody's gonna be impressed by that.

jokes.text: You know, I can't believe you just said that, Louie. It's true. Yeah, I know, but, like, incredible.
jokes.setup: Louie admits he's not a good enough actor
jokes.punchline: Bobby is amazed that Louie showed self-awareness/humility
jokes.explanation: Bobby's shock at Louie's rare moment of honest self-assessment

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm not a good enough actor.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Wait, you showed self-awareness? That's amazing!

jokes.text: Sometimes, when you're yelling at me, I can't concentrate on what you're saying 'cause what I'm concentrating on are your character traits... You know, the way your eyes pop out, and the way that vein stands out in your neck, and the spit that gets in the corner of your mouth.
jokes.setup: Bobby explaining why he wants to play Louie
jokes.punchline: Detailed catalog of Louie's unpleasant physical characteristics when angry
jokes.explanation: Uncomfortably specific and accurate description of Louie's disgusting habits

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: When Louie gets angry, his face turns this shade of purple, the veins in his neck pop out like jump ropes, his eyes bulge out so far you could use them as ashtrays, and he starts making this sound like a backed-up drain. Plus his toupee shifts about three inches to the left.

jokes.text: I'm too complex a person. You can't be me.
jokes.setup: Bobby wants to impersonate Louie
jokes.punchline: Louie's inflated sense of his own complexity
jokes.explanation: Louie's delusions of depth and sophistication are perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I'm gonna impersonate you, Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You? Impersonate me? That's impossible.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Why? How hard can it be?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Because I'm a very complex man. You'd need years of study to understand the depths of my personality.

jokes.text: You can't talk like me. You can't talk like me.
jokes.setup: Louie insisting Bobby can't imitate him
jokes.punchline: Immediately repeats himself, undermining his claim to complexity
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic structure where Louie immediately proves himself wrong through repetition

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You can't imitate me. I'm too complex.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You can't imitate me. I'm too complex.

jokes.text: It's a weird thing. I shot up in college. They wrote it up in all the medical journals.
jokes.setup: George comments that 'Louie' looks taller
jokes.punchline: Absurd explanation about a growth spurt being medically noteworthy
jokes.explanation: Absurd medical explanation that's just plausible enough to work as a cover story

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You look taller, Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I am. I had a growth spurt.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: A growth spurt? At your age?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, the doctor said it was medically noteworthy. He's writing a paper about it.

jokes.text: Who we kidding? It wasn't so hot the first time.
jokes.setup: George says it's great to see Louie again
jokes.punchline: Brutally honest admission that their relationship was never good
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie-esque honesty that cuts through social pleasantries

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's great to see you again, Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, let's be honest — our relationship was never good.

jokes.text: But what? You're surprised I'm too big for the high chair?
jokes.setup: Sheila seems confused about who this tall person is
jokes.punchline: Direct callback to the earlier high chair humiliation story
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that brings the humiliation full circle with Louie now having the upper hand

dialogue.speaker: Sheila
dialogue.line: Who is this tall person?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That's Bobby. He grew.

dialogue.speaker: Sheila
dialogue.line: Bobby? From the high chair?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Can we please not bring that up?

jokes.text: Well, uh, that's one of the purposes of this evening. To figure it out.
jokes.setup: Sheila asks how she can make up for the prom humiliation
jokes.punchline: Ominous implication that revenge is the main agenda
jokes.explanation: Menacing undertone delivered in Louie's characteristic matter-of-fact style

dialogue.speaker: Sheila
dialogue.line: How can I make up for the prom humiliation?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You can't make up for it. But you can get even.

jokes.text: Till I stand, and you hear the rattle of $70 in change.
jokes.setup: Alex noting he doesn't look like a cab driver in his jacket
jokes.punchline: The giveaway is the sound of all the change he carries
jokes.explanation: Good observational detail about the reality of cab driving

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: The giveaway is the sound of all the change he carries

jokes.text: Haven't you heard? The news is full of it. Packs of wild dogs roaming the streets. I just thought you might want someone along to hurl his body at them.
jokes.setup: Alex offers to walk Beverly home
jokes.punchline: Absurd excuse about wild dogs, offering himself as dog food
jokes.explanation: Creative and desperate pickup attempt with absurd premise and self-sacrifice

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I'll walk you home.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Oh, that's not necessary.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: No, I insist. There are wild dogs in this neighborhood. But don't worry — if we run into any, I'll let them eat me first. I'm much more tender.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: That's... very thoughtful of you.

jokes.text: And they say rejection isn't fun.
jokes.setup: Beverly rejects Alex again
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic comment about enjoying repeated rejection
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective sarcastic response to repeated romantic failure

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You know, I'm beginning to enjoy this. There's something about repeated rejection that really brings out the best in me.

jokes.text: Alex, I'd love to come. I'd like you to come, but I know how you feel about cab drivers, and I respect you. I respect you, Beverly. I really do.
jokes.setup: Beverly wants to come to the reunion
jokes.punchline: Alex passive-aggressively uses her own prejudice against cab drivers to exclude her
jokes.explanation: Satisfying moment where Alex gets subtle revenge using Beverly's own words against her

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: I'd love to come to the reunion.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm sorry, Beverly. We can't have you there.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, it's a cab drivers' reunion. And you know how you feel about cab drivers.

jokes.text: Oh, great. They promoted you from busboy.
jokes.setup: Stanley brags about being a waiter at an upscale restaurant
jokes.punchline: Dismissive comment assuming he worked his way up from the bottom
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie-style putdown that deflates Stanley's professional pride

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, that's nice Stanley. So what'd you start out as — a busboy?

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: No, I was a waiter right from the beginning.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: A waiter? Come on, nobody starts out as a waiter at a fancy place like that. You gotta work your way up from busboy, then dishwasher...

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: Well, I did.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Sure you did. And I'm the King of England.

jokes.text: That means that I make $20,000 a year. That's just in salary. I make another $40,000 in tips, of which I declare about $10,000, which gives me the equivalent of about $100,000 a year.
jokes.setup: Stanley trying to impress fake Louie with his income
jokes.punchline: Inadvertently confesses to tax evasion while bragging
jokes.explanation: Stanley's desperation to impress leads him to incriminate himself

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: You know, Louie, I make pretty good money driving this cab. Last year I cleared forty grand.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Forty grand? That's not bad.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's what I reported to the IRS anyway.

jokes.text: Well, not nearly as good, I'm afraid to say. I've only got a civil service job. I'm an auditor for the Internal Revenue Service.
jokes.setup: Stanley just confessed to tax evasion
jokes.punchline: Fake Louie reveals he works for the IRS
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup and payoff - Stanley walks directly into a trap of his own making

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Tax evasion? Stanley, that's a federal crime!

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: I know, I know. I panicked.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Well, you should have. Because I'm not really Louie De Palma.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: What? Who are you?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I work for the IRS. You're under arrest.

jokes.text: Not a good night for you, is it, Stan? Especially with you being on your way to jail and everything.
jokes.setup: Stanley just learned fake Louie is an IRS auditor
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of Stanley going to jail
jokes.explanation: Perfectly casual delivery of devastating consequences

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: An IRS auditor? Oh no... I'm going to jail.

jokes.text: Ooh! You even spilled something on your tie. Where? There.
jokes.setup: Stanley is panicked about going to jail
jokes.punchline: Fake Louie does the same nose-flick trick Stanley used to do to real Louie
jokes.explanation: Perfect revenge moment that brings the episode full circle with poetic justice

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: I'm going to jail! I'm going to jail!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Hey, don't worry about it. You'll be fine.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: Fine?! I'll be sharing a cell with some guy named Bruno!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Look, I know a guy. He can help you out.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: You do?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'll give him a call. Don't worry about it.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: Oh, thank you, Louie! Thank you!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Forget about it.

jokes.text: You are quite a man, Louie De Palma. I figure you're about 100 proof yourself, Sheila.
jokes.setup: Sheila compliments fake Louie
jokes.punchline: He responds with alcohol-strength metaphor
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay but not particularly memorable or clever

dialogue.speaker: Sheila
dialogue.line: You're a real man, Louie. Strong, decisive.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby as Louie
dialogue.line: Thanks, Sheila. I'm 200 proof.

jokes.text: That was Stanley Tarsus. Him? He was... Oh-ho! Bobby did it. Bobby did it. It really worked. I never thought it would, 'cause it was the craziest stunt in the world.
jokes.setup: Real Louie sees Stanley fleeing in panic
jokes.punchline: His amazement that the crazy plan actually worked
jokes.explanation: Louie's genuine surprise and delight at the success of the scheme

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Well, I'll be. The crazy plan actually worked.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Stanley made it out. I can't believe it.

jokes.text: All these mo-mos trying to learn how to do my steps, wishing they were with my girl.
jokes.setup: Louie watching the reunion dance
jokes.punchline: His inflated sense of his own dancing prowess and romantic success
jokes.explanation: Louie's delusional confidence about his dancing and romantic appeal

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Look at me out there. I'm a natural. The ladies can't keep their eyes off me.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Back in my day, I was the king of the dance floor. Still got it, too.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You see that one looking at me? She wants a piece of this. They all do.

jokes.text: I just want to touch Sheila's hair. I've always wanted to do that ever since high school. Don't do it, Louie. Why not? What harm could it do?
jokes.setup: Louie wants to fulfill his high school fantasy of touching Sheila's hair
jokes.punchline: Alex's warning suggests this will go badly
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup for impending disaster with Alex as the voice of reason

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Louie, you can't just go up and touch a woman's hair without permission.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Why not? I've been dreaming about this since high school.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Because she'll probably slap you, and then you'll have a lawsuit on your hands.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: A lawsuit? For touching hair?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Louie, trust me on this one. Just ask first.

jokes.text: Oh! Whoa! Whoa!
jokes.setup: Louie touches Sheila's hair against advice
jokes.punchline: Chaos erupts (presumably her wig comes off or similar disaster)
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy payoff that delivers on the setup's promise of disaster

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Hey, nice hair.

dialogue.speaker: Sheila
dialogue.line: Don't touch it.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm just complimenting you.

dialogue.speaker: Sheila
dialogue.line: I said don't touch it!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Relax, I'm barely—

jokes.text: I don't know about anybody else, but I'm really enjoying myself! Thank you!
jokes.setup: Chaos has erupted at the reunion
jokes.punchline: Louie is thrilled by the mayhem he's caused
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie moment - finding joy in chaos and completely missing social cues

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Look at this! Look at all this beautiful chaos! This is what I live for!

tmdb_overview: When Louie hesitates to attend his high-school reunion, Bobby offers to take his place.
insight: Early Taxi trades setup strength for character-driven beats at 2.5 jokes per minute.
